stem: What criteria differentiate acute bacterial sinusitis from viral upper respiratory infection?

choices.text: Positive CT scan of sinuses
choices.feedback: Imaging is NOT recommended for uncomplicated acute sinusitis — CT abnormalities are present in >80% of viral URIs. Clinical criteria are used for diagnosis.

choices.text: Symptoms lasting >10 days without improvement, OR severe onset (fever ≥39°C + purulent drainage for 3-4 days), OR double-worsening
choices.feedback: Correct! IDSA criteria for bacterial sinusitis: (1) Persistent symptoms ≥ 10 days without improvement (most common), (2) Severe onset: fever ≥ 39°C + purulent nasal discharge for ≥ 3-4 consecutive days, (3) Double-worsening: initial improvement followed by new fever, headache, or increased discharge after 5-6 days. Viral URIs typically improve within 7-10 days. Imaging NOT recommended for uncomplicated acute sinusitis.

choices.text: Any facial pain lasting more than 24 hours
choices.feedback: Facial pain alone is nonspecific and present in viral URIs, tension headaches, and other conditions. Duration and pattern of symptoms are more important.

choices.text: Presence of nasal congestion alone
choices.feedback: Nasal congestion is present in both viral URI and bacterial sinusitis. More specific criteria are needed.

explanation: Best answer: Symptoms lasting >10 days without improvement, OR severe onset (fever ≥39°C + purulent drainage for 3-4 days), OR double-worsening.
auditNote: Batch 25: Verified accurate

stem: What is the first-line antibiotic for acute bacterial sinusitis?

choices.text: TMP-SMX
choices.feedback: TMP-SMX is no longer recommended due to high resistance rates among sinusitis pathogens (up to 40% of S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae).

choices.text: Ciprofloxacin
choices.feedback: Ciprofloxacin has poor pneumococcal coverage. Respiratory FQs (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) are alternatives but not first-line.

choices.text: Amoxicillin
choices.feedback: Correct! IDSA guidelines: first-line = amoxicillin-clavulanate (standard or high-dose: 2g BID for high resistance areas). Covers the big three pathogens: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae (beta-lactamase producer → clavulanate needed), M. catarrhalis. Duration: 5-7 days for adults (vs older 10-14 day recommendation). Alternatives for penicillin allergy: doxycycline or respiratory fluoroquinolone. Amoxicillin alone is second choice (doesn't cover beta-lactamase producers).

choices.text: Azithromycin
choices.feedback: Macrolides are NOT recommended for sinusitis due to high S. pneumoniae resistance rates (30%+).

explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 25: Verified accurate

stem: What complication of sinusitis requires emergent evaluation and treatment?

choices.text: Anosmia lasting 1 week
choices.feedback: Temporary anosmia during sinusitis is common and typically resolves. It does not constitute an emergency.

choices.text: Orbital cellulitis from ethmoid sinusitis (Chandler classification)
choices.feedback: Correct! Sinusitis complications requiring emergent evaluation: Orbital (most common): preseptal/orbital cellulitis, subperiosteal abscess, orbital abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis — from ethmoid sinusitis (paper-thin lamina papyracea). Red flags: proptosis, restricted extraocular movements, vision changes, fever. Intracranial: meningitis, epidural/subdural abscess, brain abscess — from frontal sinusitis. All require CT with contrast, IV antibiotics, and possible surgical drainage.

choices.text: Post-nasal drip
choices.feedback: Post-nasal drip is a common symptom of sinusitis but is not a complication requiring emergent intervention.

choices.text: Mild headache with nasal congestion
choices.feedback: Mild headache is a common sinusitis symptom, not a complication requiring emergent care.

explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 25: Verified accurate

stem: What adjunctive therapy is recommended for all acute sinusitis (viral and bacterial)?

choices.text: Systemic corticosteroids for all patients
choices.feedback: Systemic steroids may be considered for severe symptoms or nasal polyps but are NOT recommended for all sinusitis patients.

choices.text: Prophylactic antibiotics for 6 months
choices.feedback: Long-term prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended for acute sinusitis. They may be considered only for recurrent acute bacterial sinusitis after specialist evaluation.

choices.text: Oral decongestants for 4 weeks
choices.feedback: Oral decongestants may provide short-term relief but prolonged use is not recommended.

choices.text: Intranasal saline irrigation and intranasal corticosteroids
choices.feedback: Correct! Recommended adjuncts for all sinusitis: Saline irrigation (neti pot, squeeze bottle): mechanical clearance of mucus and pathogens, reduces inflammation, improves mucociliary clearance. Intranasal corticosteroids (fluticasone, mometasone): reduce mucosal edema, improve sinus drainage, reduce symptoms. Both are safe and well-tolerated. Additional: analgesics for pain, topical decongestants (≤3 days to avoid rebound). NOT recommended: oral decongestants long-term, antihistamines (unless allergic component), mucolytics (limited evidence).

explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 25: Verified accurate

stem: A patient has had 4 episodes of acute bacterial sinusitis in the past year. What workup is indicated?

choices.text: CT sinuses, nasal endoscopy, consider allergy testing and immunoglobulin levels
choices.feedback: Correct! Recurrent acute bacterial sinusitis (RABRS: ≥4 episodes/year with symptom-free intervals) workup: CT sinuses (non-contrast — look for anatomic abnormalities, polyps, mucosal thickening), nasal endoscopy (polyps, septal deviation, middle meatus disease), allergy evaluation (skin testing/IgE), immunodeficiency screen (IgG subclasses, IgA, vaccine response titers — especially if recurrent respiratory infections), consider ciliary dysfunction testing, GERD evaluation. Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) if anatomic obstruction identified.

choices.text: MRI brain to rule out tumor
choices.feedback: MRI brain is not the first-line workup for recurrent sinusitis. CT sinuses with nasal endoscopy is the appropriate imaging.

choices.text: No workup needed — continue treating each episode
choices.feedback: Recurrent sinusitis (≥4 episodes/year) warrants investigation for underlying causes.

choices.text: Empiric long-term antibiotics without investigation
choices.feedback: Long-term empiric antibiotics without identifying the underlying cause is inappropriate and promotes resistance.

explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 25: Verified accurate

stem: A 4-year-old child presents with acute stridor, drooling, high fever, and a toxic appearance. The child is sitting upright in a tripod position and refuses to swallow. A lateral neck radiograph shows the 'thumbprint sign.' Which is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Direct laryngoscopy at bedside to visualize the epiglottis
choices.feedback: Bedside laryngoscopy in a child with suspected epiglottitis is dangerous — it can trigger laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction. Visualization should only occur in the OR where emergent surgical airway is immediately available.

choices.text: Immediate preparation for airway management in the operating room
choices.feedback: This presentation (stridor, drooling, fever, tripod positioning, thumbprint sign) is classic epiglottitis. The priority is securing the airway in a controlled environment (OR) with anesthesia and ENT/surgery backup. Agitation from examination or IV placement can precipitate complete obstruction.

choices.text: Nebulized racemic epinephrine and systemic dexamethasone
choices.feedback: Nebulized epinephrine and steroids are the treatment for viral croup (subglottic disease), not epiglottitis (supraglottic disease). The thumbprint sign, drooling, and toxic appearance distinguish epiglottitis from croup.

choices.text: CT of the neck with IV contrast to evaluate for abscess
choices.feedback: Imaging delays airway management in a child with impending obstruction. CT requires the child to lie flat, which can worsen obstruction. Airway stabilization takes absolute priority over additional imaging.

auditNote: Batch 31: Verified accurate

stem: A 22-year-old man presents with severe sore throat, trismus, muffled 'hot potato' voice, and uvular deviation to the left. CT shows a rim-enhancing collection in the right peritonsillar space. After IV antibiotics, what is the definitive treatment?

choices.text: Needle aspiration or incision and drainage of the peritonsillar abscess
choices.feedback: Peritonsillar abscess requires drainage — either needle aspiration or I&D — in addition to antibiotics. Antibiotics alone have high failure rates. Needle aspiration is often first-line as it is less invasive; I&D is performed if aspiration fails or the abscess is large. Uvula deviates AWAY from the abscess.

choices.text: Emergent tonsillectomy (quinsy tonsillectomy)
choices.feedback: Quinsy tonsillectomy is reserved for recurrent peritonsillar abscesses or failed drainage. First-line treatment is needle aspiration or I&D, which is simpler, lower risk, and effective in most cases.

choices.text: IV clindamycin alone for 7-10 days
choices.feedback: Antibiotics alone are insufficient for an established peritonsillar abscess. While appropriate antibiotics are important adjuncts, the collection must be drained to achieve resolution and prevent complications like parapharyngeal space extension.

choices.text: Oral steroids and warm salt water gargles with outpatient follow-up
choices.feedback: A formed abscess requires procedural drainage. While steroids may reduce inflammation, conservative management without drainage risks complications including airway compromise, parapharyngeal extension, and Lemierre syndrome.

auditNote: Batch 31: Verified accurate

stem: A 60-year-old man on warfarin presents with unilateral profuse epistaxis from the posterior nasal cavity that does not respond to anterior nasal packing and direct pressure for 20 minutes. His INR is 4.2. Which is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Emergent surgical ligation of the external carotid artery
choices.feedback: External carotid ligation is a last resort for life-threatening epistaxis refractory to all other measures. The stepwise approach is anterior packing → posterior packing → angiographic embolization → surgical ligation.

choices.text: Posterior nasal packing and INR reversal with vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma
choices.feedback: Refractory posterior epistaxis requires posterior packing (balloon catheter or gauze) plus reversal of the coagulopathy. The supratherapeutic INR (4.2) is a major contributing factor. Posterior packing controls the bleeding mechanically while coagulation is corrected. All patients with posterior packs require ICU monitoring.

choices.text: Continuation of anterior packing with topical oxymetazoline spray
choices.feedback: Anterior packing has already failed for 20 minutes. Posterior bleeding sources (typically from the sphenopalatine artery) require posterior packing. Continued anterior measures will not address a posterior source.

choices.text: Observation with head of bed elevation and ice packs
choices.feedback: Active posterior epistaxis on anticoagulation is not appropriate for observation. This is a potentially life-threatening situation requiring posterior packing and coagulopathy reversal.

auditNote: Batch 31: Verified accurate

stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset vertigo, nausea, nystagmus, and difficulty walking. The head impulse test is normal, nystagmus is direction-changing, and the test of skew reveals vertical misalignment. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
choices.feedback: BPPV causes brief episodes triggered by position changes with characteristic torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike testing. It does not cause persistent vertigo, skew deviation, or direction-changing nystagmus.

choices.text: Ménière disease
choices.feedback: Ménière presents with episodic vertigo plus hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. It would not cause a positive skew deviation or direction-changing nystagmus — those are central signs.

choices.text: Vestibular neuritis
choices.feedback: Vestibular neuritis causes a positive (abnormal) head impulse test, unidirectional nystagmus, and no skew deviation. All three HINTS findings here point away from a peripheral cause.

choices.text: Central vertigo, most likely a posterior circulation stroke
choices.feedback: The HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) differentiates central from peripheral vertigo. A positive skew deviation (vertical misalignment), direction-changing nystagmus, and normal head impulse test all point to a CENTRAL cause. This triad has higher sensitivity for posterior fossa stroke than early MRI.

auditNote: Batch 31: Verified accurate

stem: A 7-year-old child presents after inserting a small disc battery into her right nostril 2 hours ago. On exam, the battery is visible in the anterior nasal cavity. What makes this a true ENT emergency compared to other nasal foreign bodies?

choices.text: The metallic composition causes immediate anaphylaxis from nickel allergy
choices.feedback: While nickel allergy exists, anaphylaxis from a nasal battery is not the concern. The emergency is tissue necrosis from the electrical current and alkali production at the battery surfaces.

choices.text: Battery current causes liquefaction necrosis of the nasal mucosa and septum within hours
choices.feedback: Disc batteries generate an electrical current that causes alkaline liquefaction necrosis through the mucosa, cartilage, and bone. Septal perforation can occur within 2-4 hours. This is the only nasal foreign body that requires truly emergent removal — other objects can be managed more electively.

choices.text: Disc batteries expand with moisture and can obstruct the nasopharynx
choices.feedback: Batteries do not significantly expand. The danger is chemical and electrical tissue destruction, not mechanical obstruction. The urgency relates to the progressive necrosis that begins within hours of mucosal contact.

choices.text: Battery acid directly dissolves the nasal cartilage through acidic corrosion
choices.feedback: The mechanism is alkaline liquefaction necrosis (not acidic). The battery generates hydroxide ions at the negative pole, creating a highly alkaline environment that causes progressive tissue destruction.

auditNote: Batch 31: Verified accurate

stem: A 22-year-old man presents with 5 days of progressive sore throat, worsening over the last 24 hours despite oral antibiotics. He now has severe unilateral left-sided throat pain, trismus (difficulty opening his mouth), a muffled "hot potato" voice, drooling, and odynophagia. He is unable to swallow his own saliva.Vital SignsTemp 39.1°CHR 108ExamOropharynx Left peritonsillar bulge with uvular deviation to the RIGHT (away from abscess); left tonsil displaced mediallyTrismus Mouth opening limited to 2 cmCervical LN Left-sided tender jugulodigastric lymphadenopathy What is the diagnosis and definitive treatment?

choices.text: Severe pharyngitis — continue oral antibiotics and supportive care
Peritonsillar abscess (PTA) — needle aspiration or incision and drainage (I&D) of the abscess is required (antibiotics
Epiglottitis — secure airway immediately
Infectious mononucleosis with tonsillar enlargement — supportive care
Ludwig angina — floor of mouth infection requiring emergent airway management

explanation:
Correct Answer: Peritonsillar abscess (PTA): most common deep neck space infection. Develops as a complication of tonsillitis/pharyngitis when infection spreads beyond the tonsillar capsule into the peritonsillar space. Key findings: unilateral peritonsillar bulge, uvular deviation AWAY from the abscess, trismus (pterygoid muscle spasm), "hot potato" voice, drooling. Treatment: antibiotics alone are INSUFFICIENT — requires needle aspiration or I&D for source control. CT with contrast if diagnosis uncertain or deep space extension suspected. Complications: parapharyngeal space extension, airway compromise, Lemierre syndrome (internal jugular vein thrombophlebitis from Fusobacterium).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Progressive sore throat despite antibiotics = treatment failure (abscess formed)
- Unilateral peritonsillar bulge + uvular deviation to opposite side = PTA hallmark
- Trismus = inflammation of medial pterygoid muscle (adjacent to peritonsillar space)
- Hot potato voice = pharyngeal/peritonsillar swelling altering resonance
- Drooling/unable to swallow = severe odynophagia from abscess pressure
- Needle aspiration or I&D = REQUIRED (antibiotics alone won't resolve walled-off abscess)

Evidence: PTA - StatPearls - Galioto NJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK519512. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Severe pharyngitis — continue antibiotics: This patient has already FAILED oral antibiotics (5 days without improvement, now worsening). The peritonsillar bulge with uvular deviation indicates abscess formation, which requires DRAINAGE. Antibiotics alone cannot penetrate a walled-off abscess and will not resolve this infection.
- Epiglottitis: Epiglottitis causes severe sore throat, drooling, and muffled voice (overlapping with PTA), but epiglottitis does NOT cause trismus, peritonsillar bulge, or uvular deviation. Epiglottitis shows a toxic-appearing patient with inspiratory stridor, tripod positioning, and thumbprint sign on lateral neck X-ray. PTA is oropharyngeal; epiglottitis is supraglottic.
- Infectious mononucleosis: EBV mononucleosis causes bilateral tonsillar enlargement with exudates, generalized lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and fatigue. It is bilateral (not unilateral), does NOT cause trismus or uvular deviation, and is managed supportively. However, mononucleosis can rarely be complicated by PTA.
- Ludwig angina: Ludwig angina is a bilateral submandibular space infection causing floor-of-mouth elevation, "bull neck" swelling, and airway compromise. The swelling is in the FLOOR OF THE MOUTH and submandibular region (not the peritonsillar area). Ludwig angina is a life-threatening airway emergency requiring emergent airway management + surgical drainage.

Reference: PTA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519512/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 72-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with profuse epistaxis from the left nostril that has been continuous for 45 minutes despite direct pressure, head-forward positioning, and topical oxymetazoline. Blood is also flowing from the oropharynx. His INR was 4.2 at last check.Vital SignsBP 188/102HR 104LabsHgb 10.8 g/dL (baseline 13.5)INR 4.8Platelets 186,000 What distinguishes this from anterior epistaxis, and what is the management?

choices.text: Continue direct pressure for another 30 minutes — all nosebleeds respond eventually
Posterior epistaxis (from sphenopalatine artery branches) —
Anterior epistaxis from Kiesselbach plexus — cauterize with silver nitrate
Do not reverse anticoagulation — stroke risk is too high
Emergent CT angiogram before any intervention

explanation:
Correct Answer: Anterior vs posterior epistaxis: Anterior (90%): from Kiesselbach plexus (Little's area, anterior nasal septum); usually responds to direct pressure + topical vasoconstrictors + anterior packing or cautery. Posterior (10%): from branches of the sphenopalatine artery (posterior nasal cavity); more dangerous, presents with posterior pharyngeal blood flow, often FAILS conservative measures. Risk factors: anticoagulation, hypertension, elderly. Management of posterior epistaxis: posterior nasal packing (double-balloon catheter or Foley + anterior pack) → requires ICU admission (posterior packs can cause: (1) airway obstruction, (2) vasovagal bradycardia/apnea via nasopulmonary reflex, (3) aspiration). Reverse anticoagulation (INR 4.8 is supratherapeutic: IV vitamin K 10mg + 4-factor PCC). Control BP.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 45 min of bleeding despite anterior measures = failed conservative management
- Blood in oropharynx = posterior source (draining posteriorly past anterior pack)
- On warfarin with INR 4.8 = supratherapeutic anticoagulation contributing to hemorrhage
- Hemoglobin dropping (13.5 → 10.8) = significant blood loss
- Posterior packing required for posterior source
- ICU monitoring for posterior pack complications (airway, cardiac)

Evidence: Epistaxis - StatPearls - Womack JP, et al. StatPearls. NBK435997. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Continue direct pressure: Direct pressure has already FAILED for 45 minutes (including oxymetazoline). Continued pressure will not control a posterior source. The bleeding site is posterior to the area compressed by direct pressure. Posterior packing is required to tamponade the sphenopalatine artery branches.
- Anterior epistaxis — cauterize: If the source were anterior (Kiesselbach plexus), anterior measures would have succeeded. Blood flowing from the oropharynx indicates a POSTERIOR source. Cauterizing the anterior septum would not reach the sphenopalatine artery branches causing the hemorrhage.
- Do not reverse anticoagulation: While anticoagulation reversal carries stroke risk in AF patients, an INR of 4.8 is SUPRATHERAPEUTIC (target 2.0-3.0 for AF). Reversal to the therapeutic range (INR 2-3) still provides stroke protection while reducing hemorrhage. Active life-threatening hemorrhage with supratherapeutic INR is an indication for urgent reversal.
- CT angiogram before intervention: While CT angiography is useful for surgical/embolization planning, it should NOT delay initial management of active hemorrhage. The priority is: stabilize hemodynamics, pack the nose, reverse anticoagulation. CTA can be obtained after initial stabilization if surgical intervention is being considered.

Reference: Epistaxis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK435997/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 14-month-old girl is brought in with 2 days of fever, irritability, and pulling at her right ear. She had a URI last week. Exam shows a bulging, erythematous, immobile right tympanic membrane with purulent effusion behind it. Left ear is normal.Vital SignsTemp 39.2°C What are the treatment guidelines, and when is watchful waiting appropriate vs immediate antibiotics?

choices.text: All children with ear infections need immediate antibiotics
Acute otitis media in a child <2 years with UNILATERAL AOM and non-severe symptoms: watchful waiting is
Watchful waiting for all children regardless of age or severity
Start azithromycin as first-line treatment
Tympanocentesis is required for all cases of AOM

explanation:
Correct Answer: AOM treatment decision tree (AAP 2013/updated guidelines): ALWAYS treat immediately: (1) Watchful waiting option: age 6-23 months with UNILATERAL non-severe OR ≥2 years with unilateral/bilateral non-severe. This child has SEVERE AOM (T 39.2°C) at 14 months → IMMEDIATE antibiotics. First-line: high-dose amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day (covers S. pneumoniae, most common pathogen). Treatment failure at 48-72h: switch to amoxicillin-clavulanate (adds beta-lactamase resistant coverage for H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 14 months old + bulging erythematous TM + purulent effusion = AOM
- Temperature 39.2°C = severe (≥39°C meets AAP severe criteria)
- Severe symptoms at <2 years = ALWAYS treat immediately (not watchful waiting)
- High-dose amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day = first-line
- URI preceding = classic sequence (viral URI → eustachian tube dysfunction → bacterial AOM)
- Immobile TM on pneumatic otoscopy = middle ear effusion (most specific finding)

Evidence: AOM - StatPearls - Danishyar A, et al. StatPearls. NBK470332. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- All children need immediate antibiotics: The AAP guidelines allow WATCHFUL WAITING for selected cases: age 6-23 months with UNILATERAL, NON-SEVERE AOM, or ≥2 years with non-severe AOM. This reduces unnecessary antibiotic use. However, this specific child has SEVERE symptoms (T ≥39°C) and IS treated immediately.
- Watchful waiting for all children: Watchful waiting is ONLY appropriate for non-severe, select cases. It is NEVER appropriate for: children <6 months, bilateral AOM in <2 years, any AOM with otorrhea, or severe symptoms (T ≥39°C or moderate-severe otalgia ≥48h). This child meets severe criteria.
- Azithromycin first-line: Azithromycin is NOT first-line for AOM. It has high resistance rates for S. pneumoniae (up to 30-40%) and provides suboptimal middle ear concentrations. First-line is HIGH-DOSE AMOXICILLIN (80-90 mg/kg/day). Azithromycin is a distant alternative only for true penicillin allergy with anaphylaxis history.
- Tympanocentesis required: Tympanocentesis is RESERVED for: neonates (<2 months), immunocompromised, failure of second-line antibiotics, severe complications (mastoiditis, intracranial), or when organism identification is needed. Routine AOM is diagnosed and treated clinically.

Reference: AOM - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470332/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 4-year-old unvaccinated boy presents with rapid-onset high fever, severe sore throat, drooling, muffled voice, and inspiratory stridor. Symptoms progressed from mild sore throat to respiratory distress over 6 hours. He is sitting upright in a tripod position (leaning forward on extended arms) with his neck extended and chin thrust forward ("sniffing position"). He appears toxic.Vital SignsTemp 40.2°CHR 156RR 36SpO2 91%ExamPosition Tripod, refuses to lie downVoice Muffled, speaks in whispersDrooling CopiousStridor Inspiratory, worseningOropharynx Do NOT examine with tongue blade in suspected epiglottitis What is the diagnosis, what imaging finding is expected, and what is the critical management principle?

choices.text: Croup — barking cough with steeple sign on X-ray
Acute epiglottitis — AIRWAY EMERGENCY;
Peritonsillar abscess — unilateral peritonsillar bulge with trismus
Retropharyngeal abscess — neck stiffness with refusal to extend neck
Foreign body aspiration — sudden choking episode with unilateral wheezing

explanation:
Correct Answer: Epiglottitis: life-threatening supraglottic infection causing rapid airway compromise. DO NOT agitate the child: no tongue depressors, no throat exam, no blood draws, no forced supine position, no separation from parent — any of these can trigger complete laryngospasm and airway obstruction. Thumbprint sign on lateral neck X-ray (swollen epiglottis obliterating vallecula). Epiglottitis vs croup: epiglottitis = rapid onset (hours), toxic-appearing, HIGH fever, drooling, muffled voice, NO cough, tripod position. Croup = gradual onset (days), BARKING cough, hoarse voice, low-grade fever, non-toxic, steeple sign. Post-Hib vaccine: epiglottitis has dramatically decreased in children (still occurs in unvaccinated and adults).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Rapid 6-hour progression = epiglottitis (croup evolves over days)
- Toxic appearance + high fever (40.2°C) = bacterial supraglottic infection
- Tripod position + sniffing position = optimizing airway (maximizing supraglottic aperture)
- Drooling = unable to swallow (supraglottic swelling)
- Muffled voice (NOT hoarse/barking) = supraglottic pathology
- Inspiratory stridor + SpO2 91% = impending airway compromise
- Unvaccinated = H. influenzae type b remains most common cause

Evidence: Epiglottitis - StatPearls - Bochner RE, et al. StatPearls. NBK430960. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Croup: Croup and epiglottitis are the critical pediatric stridor differential. Croup: GRADUAL onset (days of URI), BARKING cough (hallmark), hoarse voice, low-grade fever, NON-TOXIC appearance, steeple sign on AP X-ray. Epiglottitis: RAPID onset (hours), NO cough, muffled voice, HIGH fever, TOXIC appearance, thumbprint sign on lateral X-ray. The absence of barking cough and the toxic appearance with rapid progression rules out croup.
- Peritonsillar abscess (PTA): PTA causes unilateral peritonsillar bulge, uvular deviation, trismus, and hot potato voice. While voice changes overlap, PTA does NOT typically cause stridor, tripod positioning, or drooling (unless very large). PTA is an oropharyngeal process; epiglottitis is supraglottic. PTA patients do not appear this toxic.
- Retropharyngeal abscess: Retropharyngeal abscess causes neck stiffness with REFUSAL TO EXTEND the neck (opposite of this child who is extending and sniffing). It presents with fever, dysphagia, and widened prevertebral space on lateral X-ray. Retropharyngeal abscess tends to have a more gradual onset than epiglottitis.
- Foreign body aspiration: Foreign body aspiration causes sudden choking during eating/playing (acute event), often with unilateral wheezing or decreased breath sounds. This child has fever (40.2°C), gradual 6-hour progression from sore throat, and drooling — all inconsistent with aspiration.

Reference: Epiglottitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430960/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with 12 days of nasal congestion, purulent nasal discharge, facial pressure/pain over the maxillary area bilaterally, and post-nasal drip. She had a typical cold 2 weeks ago that seemed to improve after 5 days, then worsened again ("double sickening" pattern). She has low-grade fever today.Vital SignsTemp 38.2°CExamFace Tenderness over bilateral maxillary sinuses with percussionNasal mucosa Erythematous, purulent discharge from middle meatusTransillumination Opaque bilaterallyPharynx Post-nasal drip What distinguishes bacterial sinusitis from viral URI, and when are antibiotics indicated?

choices.text: All patients with nasal congestion and facial pressure need antibiotics
Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis (ABRS) — diagnosed CLINICALLY when symptoms persist ≥10 DAYS without
CT sinus is required before starting antibiotics
Azithromycin is first-line for sinusitis
Symptoms lasting 5 days warrant antibiotic treatment

explanation:
Correct Answer: ABRS vs viral URI: viral URI typically improves by 7-10 days. ABRS is diagnosed CLINICALLY (no imaging needed) by any of 3 criteria: (1) Symptoms ≥10 days without improvement, (2) "Double sickening" (improvement then worsening around day 5-7), (3) Severe onset (high fever ≥39°C + purulent discharge ≥3 days). This patient has "double sickening" at day 12 = ABRS. First-line: amoxicillin-clavulanate (NOT amoxicillin alone, due to increasing beta-lactamase producing H. influenzae). NOT azithromycin (high resistance rates for S. pneumoniae). CT sinus: only for suspected complications (orbital, intracranial), chronic sinusitis, or surgical planning. Adjunctive: nasal saline irrigation (evidence-based), intranasal corticosteroids (reduce inflammation).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 12 days of symptoms = exceeds 10-day threshold
- Double sickening pattern = initial URI improved then worsened = classic ABRS
- Purulent discharge from middle meatus = maxillary/anterior ethmoid drainage pathway
- Maxillary tenderness = maxillary sinusitis (most commonly involved sinus)
- Low-grade fever = supports bacterial infection
- Clinical diagnosis: no CT needed for uncomplicated ABRS

Evidence: Acute Sinusitis - IDSA/StatPearls - Rosenfeld RM, et al. StatPearls. NBK547701. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- All nasal congestion needs antibiotics: The vast majority (90-98%) of rhinosinusitis is VIRAL and self-limited. Prescribing antibiotics for all nasal congestion contributes to antibiotic resistance without benefit. Antibiotics are only indicated when ABRS criteria are met: ≥10 days without improvement, double sickening, or severe onset.
- CT sinus required before antibiotics: CT sinus is NOT needed for uncomplicated ABRS. CT is abnormal in 87% of VIRAL URIs (mucosal thickening is nonspecific), so it cannot distinguish bacterial from viral. CT is reserved for: suspected complications (orbital/intracranial extension), chronic sinusitis evaluation, or surgical planning.
- Azithromycin is first-line: Azithromycin (Z-pack) is NOT recommended as first-line for sinusitis due to high S. pneumoniae resistance rates (30-40% in many areas). IDSA guidelines specifically recommend against macrolides. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is first-line.
- 5 days of symptoms warrants antibiotics: Most viral URIs last 7-10 days. Symptoms at day 5 are typically still within the viral window. The 10-day threshold (without improvement) was established because most viral URIs are improving by day 10. Treating at day 5 would result in unnecessary antibiotics for viral infections.

stem: A 55-year-old man presents with sudden loss of hearing in his right ear that he noticed upon waking this morning. He has no ear pain, no trauma, no recent URI. He reports a fullness sensation and mild tinnitus in the affected ear. Exam: TMs are normal bilaterally with no effusion.ExamWeber test Lateralizes to LEFT (unaffected) earRinne test Right ear: air conduction > bone conduction (positive Rinne); Left ear: AC > BC (positive Rinne)Otoscopy Normal TMs bilaterally, no effusion, no cerumen impactionAudiogram0 R1 i2 g3 h4 t5  6 e7 a8 r9 :10  11 >12 313 014  15 d16 B17  18 s19 e20 n21 s22 o23 r24 i25 n26 e27 u28 r29 a30 l31  32 h33 e34 a35 r36 i37 n38 g39  40 l41 o42 s43 s44  45 a46 c47 r48 o49 s50 s51  52 353  54 c55 o56 n57 t58 i59 g60 u61 o62 u63 s64  65 f66 r67 e68 q69 u70 e71 n72 c73 i74 e75 s What is the diagnosis, and why is this considered an emergency?

choices.text: Conductive hearing loss from otitis media — antibiotics
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) — an OTOLOGIC EMERGENCY;
Cerumen impaction — remove cerumen and recheck
Presbycusis — age-related hearing loss that is gradual and bilateral
Meniere disease — vertigo with hearing loss and tinnitus

explanation:
Correct Answer: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL): ≥30 dB loss over at least 3 contiguous frequencies occurring within 72 hours. This is an OTOLOGIC EMERGENCY because treatment within 2 weeks (ideally Tuning fork interpretation: Weber lateralizes to the UNAFFECTED ear (damaged cochlea cannot perceive the vibration) = sensorineural loss in the affected ear. Conductive loss: Weber lateralizes TO the affected ear (blocked external/middle ear amplifies bone conduction). Treatment: high-dose oral prednisone (1 mg/kg/day x 10-14 days with taper) or intratympanic dexamethasone. MRI with gadolinium: rule out vestibular schwannoma (retrocochlear pathology) in ALL cases of unilateral SSNHL (~1% have acoustic neuroma). Most SSNHL is idiopathic (probably viral cochleitis or vascular occlusion).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sudden unilateral hearing loss upon waking = SSNHL until proven otherwise
- Weber to LEFT (unaffected) ear = sensorineural loss on RIGHT
- Rinne positive bilaterally (AC>BC) = no conductive component
- Normal TMs = no middle ear pathology
- Audiogram: >30 dB sensorineural loss across 3 frequencies = meets SSNHL criteria
- Emergency: treatment window is narrow (best within 72 hours, must be within 2 weeks)

Evidence: SSNHL - StatPearls - Kuhn M, et al. StatPearls. NBK560647. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Conductive hearing loss: In conductive hearing loss, Weber lateralizes TO the affected ear (bone conduction is amplified relative to blocked air conduction), and Rinne is NEGATIVE on the affected side (BC>AC). This patient has Weber to the OPPOSITE ear and positive Rinne bilaterally = sensorineural, not conductive loss.
- Cerumen impaction: Cerumen impaction causes conductive hearing loss (Weber to affected side, negative Rinne) and is visible on otoscopy. This patient has normal otoscopy and sensorineural hearing loss pattern, ruling out cerumen impaction.
- Presbycusis: Presbycusis is age-related hearing loss that is BILATERAL, SYMMETRIC, GRADUAL (over years), and affects HIGH frequencies first. This patient has SUDDEN, UNILATERAL loss. Presbycusis is never sudden and never unilateral.
- Meniere disease: Meniere disease causes episodes of VERTIGO (usually severe, lasting minutes to hours), fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. While some features overlap (hearing loss, tinnitus, fullness), Meniere has EPISODIC vertigo as the hallmark. This patient has no significant vertigo. However, Meniere CAN present as SSNHL in some cases, so MRI is still needed to rule out schwannoma.

Reference: SSNHL - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560647/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with 4 days of fever, neck stiffness, refusal to extend his neck, and decreased oral intake. He had a URI 1 week ago. He holds his neck in slight flexion and resists extension. Exam shows posterior pharyngeal wall bulging. He has been drooling.Vital SignsTemp 39.4°CHR 148RR 28ImagingLateral neck X-ray Prevertebral soft tissue width at C2 = 10 mm (normal CT neck with contrast 3.5 cm rim-enhancing fluid collection in the retropharyngeal space, anterior to C2-C4 vertebral bodies What is the diagnosis, and what complication makes this life-threatening?

choices.text: Peritonsillar abscess — trismus with uvular deviation
Retropharyngeal abscess (RPA) — a deep neck space infection that is an AIRWAY EMERGENCY;
Meningitis — neck stiffness with fever
Cervical lymphadenitis — enlarged tender neck node
Epiglottitis — supraglottic swelling with stridor

explanation:
Correct Answer: Retropharyngeal abscess (RPA): deep neck space infection posterior to the pharynx, anterior to prevertebral fascia. Most common in children KEY FINDING: neck stiffness with REFUSAL TO EXTEND the neck (distinguishes from meningitis where neck resists FLEXION). Life-threatening complication: the retropharyngeal space is a potential space that extends from the skull base to the posterior mediastinum → infection can descend into the mediastinum (descending necrotizing mediastinitis, 40-50% mortality). Other complications: airway obstruction, aspiration (if abscess ruptures), internal jugular vein thrombosis (Lemierre syndrome), carotid artery erosion. CT neck with IV contrast: rim-enhancing fluid collection is diagnostic. Widened prevertebral space on lateral X-ray: C2 >7mm or C6 >14mm in children (>22mm in adults).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 3-year-old after URI + 4 days of fever = typical RPA demographic and timeline
- Refusal to EXTEND neck (NOT resist flexion, which would be meningitis)
- Posterior pharyngeal wall bulging = retropharyngeal mass effect
- Drooling = pharyngeal compression impairing swallowing
- Widened prevertebral space on lateral X-ray = retropharyngeal process
- CT: rim-enhancing fluid collection = abscess (vs phlegmon which doesn't rim-enhance)

Evidence: Retropharyngeal Abscess - StatPearls - Vieira F, et al. StatPearls. NBK441873. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Peritonsillar abscess: PTA causes UNILATERAL peritonsillar bulging with uvular deviation, trismus, and hot potato voice. The bulging in PTA is LATERAL (peritonsillar), not POSTERIOR (retropharyngeal). PTA is rare in 3-year-olds (peak age 20-40). PTA does not cause refusal to extend the neck.
- Meningitis: This is the critical distinction: meningitis causes neck stiffness with resistance to FLEXION (Brudzinski and Kernig signs). RPA causes resistance to EXTENSION (because extension compresses the retropharyngeal abscess against the vertebral bodies). Also, meningitis does not cause posterior pharyngeal bulging or widened prevertebral space.
- Cervical lymphadenitis: Cervical lymphadenitis causes a tender, enlarged external neck node, often with overlying erythema. It does NOT cause posterior pharyngeal wall bulging, widened prevertebral space, or neck extension restriction. Lymphadenitis is external; RPA is a deep space infection.
- Epiglottitis: Epiglottitis causes rapid-onset (hours) stridor, drooling, tripod positioning, and SUPRAGLOTTIC swelling (thumbprint sign). While drooling overlaps, epiglottitis progresses much faster (hours vs days), causes inspiratory stridor prominently, and involves the epiglottis (not retropharyngeal space). Lateral X-ray would show thumbprint sign, not widened prevertebral space.

Reference: Retropharyngeal Abscess - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441873/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the ED after a sudden-onset choking episode while eating peanuts 3 hours ago. He had a brief coughing spell that partially resolved. Now he has persistent unilateral wheezing on the RIGHT side. He is in no acute distress, speaking in full sentences, and saturation is 96%.Vital SignsRR 28SpO2 96%ExamAuscultation Decreased breath sounds + expiratory wheezing on RIGHT; clear on LEFTCXR (inspiratory) NormalCXR (expiratory/bilateral decubitus) Air trapping in RIGHT lung (right lung remains hyperinflated on expiration) What is the diagnosis, and what finding confirms the location?

choices.text: Bilateral asthma exacerbation — treat with albuterol
Foreign body aspiration in the RIGHT mainstem bronchus — UNILATERAL wheezing in a toddler with a
Pneumonia — unilateral findings suggest consolidation
Croup — viral laryngotracheobronchitis causing stridor
Normal inspiratory CXR rules out foreign body

explanation:
Correct Answer: Foreign body aspiration: peak age 1-3 years (oral exploration + immature airway protective reflexes + foods like peanuts, grapes, hot dogs). RIGHT mainstem bronchus is more common (right is shorter, wider, and more vertical). Key clinical finding: UNILATERAL wheezing (asthma is bilateral). Imaging: inspiratory CXR is often NORMAL (most aspirated objects are radiolucent); expiratory or bilateral decubitus films show the diagnostic AIR TRAPPING (the affected lung fails to deflate because the FB acts as a ball valve). Treatment: rigid bronchoscopy (allows ventilation during the procedure + larger working channel for foreign body extraction). Not flexible bronchoscopy in small children. NEVER perform blind finger sweeps in children (may push FB deeper).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Toddler (2 years) + choking episode while eating peanuts = high-risk scenario for aspiration
- Sudden onset = acute event (not chronic like asthma)
- UNILATERAL right-sided wheezing = foreign body (asthma = bilateral)
- Normal inspiratory CXR = does NOT rule out FB (most FBs are radiolucent)
- Expiratory air trapping on RIGHT = ball-valve obstruction in right bronchus
- RIGHT mainstem bronchus = more common location (anatomic factors)

Evidence: Foreign Body Aspiration - StatPearls - Srivastava R, et al. StatPearls. NBK531480. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Asthma: Asthma causes BILATERAL wheezing, not unilateral. While asthma is common in this age group, the sudden onset during eating with a choking episode and strictly unilateral findings makes foreign body aspiration the top diagnosis. Treating with bronchodilators may mask the true diagnosis.
- Pneumonia: Pneumonia can cause unilateral findings, but the acute onset with a choking episode while eating is classic for aspiration. Pneumonia develops over hours-days with fever, cough, and tachypnea. Air trapping on expiratory films is characteristic of FB obstruction, not consolidation from pneumonia.
- Croup: Croup causes STRIDOR (not wheezing), has a barking cough, and causes UPPER airway obstruction (not lower/bronchial). Croup is bilateral, caused by parainfluenza virus, and treated with dexamethasone + nebulized epinephrine. This child has unilateral LOWER airway findings.
- Normal inspiratory CXR rules out FB: This is DANGEROUS. Up to 25-40% of foreign body aspirations have a NORMAL inspiratory CXR because most aspirated objects (foods) are radiolucent. EXPIRATORY or decubitus films are essential to demonstrate air trapping. If clinical suspicion is high, proceed to bronchoscopy even with normal imaging.

Reference: FB Aspiration - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK531480/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset hearing loss in her left ear that she noticed upon waking this morning. She describes a feeling of fullness in the ear and constant tinnitus. She has no ear pain, no discharge, and no vertigo. Otoscopic exam of both ears is completely normal (normal tympanic membranes, no effusion, no cerumen impaction).ExamOtoscopy Normal TMs bilaterallyWeber test Lateralizes to the RIGHT (unaffected ear)Rinne test Air conduction > bone conduction bilaterally (normal pattern)Audiogram Left ear: ≥30 dB loss across 3 contiguous frequencies (sensorineural pattern) What is the diagnosis, and what is the time-sensitive treatment?

choices.text: Cerumen impaction — remove earwax and hearing will return
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) — otologic EMERGENCY defined as ≥30 dB loss over 3 contiguous
Otitis media with effusion — watchful waiting
Meniere disease — episodic vertigo with hearing loss
Hearing loss will resolve spontaneously without treatment in all cases

explanation:
Correct Answer: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL): otologic EMERGENCY. Defined as ≥30 dB hearing loss over ≥3 contiguous frequencies developing within 72 hours. Most cases are idiopathic (65-90%); proposed mechanisms: viral inflammation, vascular compromise, autoimmune. Weber test: lateralizes to the UNAFFECTED ear (in sensorineural loss, the affected cochlea can't perceive sound). In conductive loss: lateralizes to the AFFECTED ear. Treatment: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day x 10-14 days with taper) within 2 weeks of onset (earlier = better). MRI with gadolinium: rule out acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma, present in 1-3% of SSNHL). Intratympanic dexamethasone: salvage therapy if oral steroids fail, or first-line if systemic steroids contraindicated. Spontaneous recovery occurs in ~30-65%, but steroids significantly improve outcomes.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sudden unilateral hearing loss + tinnitus + fullness = SSNHL presentation
- Normal otoscopy = no conductive cause (no effusion, no cerumen, normal TM)
- Weber to RIGHT (unaffected ear) = sensorineural pattern
- Rinne normal bilaterally (AC > BC) = sensorineural, not conductive
- Audiogram confirms ≥30 dB sensorineural loss = meets SSNHL criteria
- No vertigo = less likely Meniere (which has episodic vertigo as cardinal feature)

Evidence: SSNHL - StatPearls - Stachler RJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK560900. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Cerumen impaction: Cerumen impaction causes CONDUCTIVE hearing loss (Weber lateralizes to the AFFECTED ear, Rinne shows BC > AC on affected side) and is visible on otoscopy. This patient has normal otoscopy and sensorineural pattern on tuning forks. The hearing loss is cochlear, not external canal.
- Otitis media with effusion: OME causes CONDUCTIVE hearing loss with visible middle ear effusion (air-fluid level or TM retraction/immobility on otoscopy). This patient has completely normal TMs and a sensorineural hearing loss pattern. OME does not cause sensorineural loss.
- Meniere disease: Meniere disease is characterized by the classic triad of episodic VERTIGO (>20 minutes), fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, and tinnitus. The cardinal feature is VERTIGO, which this patient does NOT have. Meniere is also typically episodic/fluctuating, not a single sudden event.
- Spontaneous resolution without treatment: While spontaneous recovery occurs in 30-65% of SSNHL cases, treatment with corticosteroids significantly improves outcomes (NNT ~6). Waiting without treatment means missing the critical early window when steroids are most effective. Given the potential for permanent hearing loss, treatment should not be withheld.

Reference: SSNHL - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560900/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 52-year-old obese man (BMI 36) presents with his wife, who reports he snores loudly every night and she has witnessed episodes where he stops breathing for 10-15 seconds before gasping and resuming breathing. He reports excessive daytime sleepiness, morning headaches, and difficulty concentrating at work. He fell asleep while driving last month. Mallampati score IV. Neck circumference is 44 cm.ExamBMI 36Neck circumference 44 cm (>40 cm = high risk)Mallampati Class IV (only hard palate visible)Oropharynx Crowded with large uvula and tonsillar hypertrophyEpworth Sleepiness Scale 18/24 (severe daytime sleepiness) What is the gold standard diagnostic test, and what severity metric guides treatment?

choices.text: Clinical diagnosis alone is sufficient — no testing needed
Polysomnography (PSG, “sleep study”) is the gold standard
Pulse oximetry overnight is equivalent to polysomnography
Start CPAP empirically without a sleep study
CT scan of the airway to measure obstruction

explanation:
Correct Answer: Obstructive sleep apnea: repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep → apneas (complete cessation ≥10 seconds) and hypopneas (partial reduction with ≥3% desaturation or arousal). Gold standard: attended in-lab polysomnography (PSG). Home sleep testing (HST) is acceptable for uncomplicated moderate-severe OSA in patients without significant comorbidities. AHI (apnea-hypopnea index): Mild 5-14/h, Moderate 15-29/h, Severe ≥30/h. Treatment: Mild: positional therapy, weight loss, oral appliance. Moderate-severe: CPAP (first-line; splints airway open with positive pressure). Surgical options: uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP), maxillomandibular advancement, hypoglossal nerve stimulator (Inspire). Complications of untreated OSA: HTN (resistant), atrial fibrillation, stroke, MI, pulmonary hypertension, type 2 DM, motor vehicle accidents (6-7x increased risk), sudden cardiac death.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Witnessed apneas (wife reports breathing pauses) = high specificity for OSA
- Excessive daytime sleepiness with Epworth 18/24 = severe
- Fell asleep driving = dangerous level of sleepiness
- BMI 36, neck circumference 44 cm, Mallampati IV = anatomic risk factors
- Snoring + observed apneas + daytime sleepiness = STOP-BANG ≥5 = high risk
- PSG needed to confirm diagnosis and determine AHI for treatment planning

Evidence: OSA - StatPearls - Slowik JM, et al. StatPearls. NBK459252. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Clinical diagnosis sufficient: While clinical suspicion can be high (STOP-BANG ≥5), the AHI from PSG or HSAT is REQUIRED to confirm the diagnosis, determine severity, and guide treatment selection. Insurance requires documented testing before prescribing CPAP. Clinical assessment alone cannot distinguish mild from severe OSA.
- Overnight oximetry equivalent to PSG: Overnight oximetry can detect oxygen desaturations but MISSES hypopneas without desaturation, cannot calculate AHI accurately, cannot differentiate central from obstructive apneas, and does not assess sleep staging or arousal index. It is a screening tool, not a diagnostic substitute for PSG.
- Empiric CPAP without sleep study: While CPAP is the likely treatment, the sleep study is needed to: (1) confirm the diagnosis objectively, (2) determine AHI severity (guides treatment), (3) titrate CPAP pressure, (4) rule out central sleep apnea (which requires different treatment), and (5) satisfy insurance/documentation requirements.
- CT airway: CT scanning can assess anatomic narrowing but does NOT provide the functional information needed for OSA diagnosis. OSA is a DYNAMIC disorder (airway collapse occurs during sleep with reduced muscle tone). Awake CT shows the static anatomy but cannot capture the sleep-related obstruction pattern.

Reference: OSA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459252/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 22-year-old man presents with 5 days of worsening sore throat, fever, and difficulty swallowing. He now has severe odynophagia, drooling, and a "hot potato" muffled voice. He can barely open his mouth. He had pharyngitis treated with amoxicillin 1 week ago.Vital SignsTemp 39.2°CHR 108ExamOropharynx Asymmetric tonsillar bulging on RIGHT, uvula deviated to LEFT, trismus (limited mouth opening), soft palate erythema and swellingNeck Right anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, tender What is the diagnosis and immediate management?

choices.text: Mononucleosis with tonsillar enlargement
Peritonsillar ABSCESS (PTA, quinsy) — complication of
Severe pharyngitis — continue amoxicillin
Retropharyngeal abscess requiring CT
Epiglottitis — emergent intubation

explanation:
Correct Answer: Peritonsillar abscess (PTA) classic triad:
1. Trismus (can't open mouth)
2. Uvular deviation (AWAY from abscess)
3. 'Hot potato' muffled voice

Additional features: unilateral tonsillar bulging, severe odynophagia, drooling, fever, recent pharyngitis.

Management: DRAINAGE is essential (needle aspiration or I&D) + IV antibiotics. Antibiotics alone are insufficient for abscess.

Distinguish from:
• Retropharyngeal abscess: pediatric, posterior pharyngeal swelling, neck stiffness
• Epiglottitis: stridor, drooling, tripod positioning, no trismus

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 5 days worsening sore throat after pharyngitis = abscess development
- Trismus = pterygoid muscle involvement (classic PTA)
- Hot potato voice = palatal swelling
- Uvula deviated LEFT = abscess on RIGHT pushing it over
- Asymmetric tonsillar bulging = abscess
- Drooling = can't swallow due to pain
- Management: drainage + IV antibiotics + ENT consult

Evidence: Peritonsillar Abscess - StatPearls - Galioto NJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK519512. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Mononucleosis: Mononucleosis can cause tonsillar enlargement and exudates, but produces SYMMETRIC bilateral enlargement without trismus or uvular deviation. Mono also has posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and positive heterophile antibody. This patient's asymmetric bulging and trismus indicate abscess.
- Continue amoxicillin: This patient has FAILED outpatient antibiotic therapy and now has an abscess. Oral antibiotics alone cannot treat a PTA — DRAINAGE is required. Continuing the same antibiotic will allow progression to parapharyngeal abscess or airway compromise.
- Retropharyngeal abscess: Retropharyngeal abscess (RPA) is more common in CHILDREN, presents with neck stiffness, and shows swelling of the POSTERIOR pharyngeal wall (not peritonsillar). RPA patients may have difficulty with neck extension. This patient has anterior/lateral tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation — PTA, not RPA.
- Epiglottitis: Epiglottitis presents with STRIDOR, muffled voice, drooling, and tripod positioning. There is no TRISMUS in epiglottitis (the obstruction is supraglottic, not oropharyngeal). This patient has trismus, uvular deviation, and tonsillar bulging — PTA, not epiglottitis.

Reference: PTA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519512/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 48-year-old man woke up this morning with sudden hearing loss in his left ear. He also reports tinnitus and a sensation of fullness in that ear. There was no trauma, no URI symptoms. He has no vertigo. Otoscopy shows a normal tympanic membrane bilaterally. Weber test lateralizes to the RIGHT (unaffected ear). Rinne is air > bone bilaterally.ExamOtoscopy Normal TM bilaterally, no effusionWeber Lateralizes to RIGHT (unaffected ear)Rinne Air > bone bilaterally (normal)Whisper test Impaired left What type of hearing loss is this, and what is the time-sensitive treatment?

choices.text: Conductive hearing loss from cerumen impaction
Sudden SENSORINEURAL hearing loss (SSNHL) — this is an OTOLOGIC EMERGENCY:
Otitis media with effusion causing hearing loss
Presbycusis — age-related hearing loss
Observation without treatment — hearing will recover spontaneously

explanation:
Correct Answer: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL):
• Definition: ≥30 dB loss over ≤3 days in ≥3 frequencies
• OTOLOGIC EMERGENCY — treatment within 2 weeks, ideally 24-72h

Tuning fork tests:
• Weber: lateralizes to UNAFFECTED ear in sensorineural loss (lateralizes to AFFECTED ear in conductive loss)
• Rinne: air > bone = sensorineural (normal); bone > air = conductive

Workup: audiogram (confirm ≥30 dB), MRI with gadolinium (rule out acoustic neuroma/vestibular schwannoma — 1% of SSNHL)

Treatment: high-dose steroids (prednisone 60mg or 1mg/kg/day x 10-14 days with taper). Intratympanic steroids if systemic contraindicated.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sudden onset = SSNHL until proven otherwise
- Normal TM = not conductive (no effusion, no perforation)
- Weber to RIGHT (unaffected) = sensorineural loss on LEFT
- Rinne air > bone = sensorineural pattern
- No vertigo = less likely Meniere's or labyrinthitis
- Must get MRI to rule out acoustic neuroma
- Treatment: steroids within 72 hours for best outcome

Evidence: Sudden Sensorineural Hearing Loss - StatPearls - Chandrasekhar SS, et al. StatPearls. NBK545103. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Cerumen impaction: Cerumen impaction causes CONDUCTIVE hearing loss. Weber would lateralize to the AFFECTED ear (conductive enhances bone conduction). Rinne would be bone > air on the affected side. This patient has Weber to the unaffected ear and normal Rinne — sensorineural pattern, not conductive.
- Otitis media with effusion: OME causes CONDUCTIVE hearing loss with visible abnormality on otoscopy (fluid behind TM, air-fluid level, retracted TM). This patient has NORMAL otoscopy and a sensorineural pattern on tuning fork tests.
- Presbycusis: Presbycusis is age-related GRADUAL bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, typically high-frequency. It does not present as SUDDEN unilateral loss. This patient's acute presentation requires workup for SSNHL, not dismissal as aging.
- Observation only: SSNHL is an otologic emergency. Spontaneous recovery occurs in ~50%, but prompt steroid treatment improves recovery rates. Delaying treatment beyond 2 weeks significantly worsens prognosis. Early high-dose steroids are the standard of care.

Reference: SSNHL - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545103/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with anterior nosebleed from the left nostril that has been ongoing for 30 minutes despite applying pressure. Blood is dripping from the anterior nares. He is hemodynamically stable.Vital SignsBP 162/88HR 78LabsINR 3.8 (supratherapeutic, target 2-3)Hgb 13.2 What is the stepwise management of epistaxis?

choices.text: Immediate posterior packing is required
ANTERIOR epistaxis management (90% of nosebleeds from Kiesselbach's plexus): (1) DIRECT
Transfuse blood products first given anticoagulation
Have patient tilt head backward to stop bleeding
Cauterize both sides of septum simultaneously

explanation:
Correct Answer: Epistaxis management stepwise:
1. Direct pressure: 15-20 min, lean FORWARD
2. Topical vasoconstrictor + anesthetic
3. Visualize and identify source
4. Cautery (chemical or electric) if source visible
5. Anterior packing if cautery fails
6. Posterior packing if anterior source not found or posterior bleed
7. Correct coagulopathy

Kiesselbach's plexus: anterior septum, 90% of epistaxis. Posterior bleeds (sphenopalatine artery): more severe, older patients, harder to control.

Anticoagulation: supratherapeutic INR (3.8) should be corrected. Hold warfarin, give vitamin K (2.5-5mg PO/IV) for non-life-threatening bleeds. FFP or 4-factor PCC for severe bleeds.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 30 min bleeding despite pressure = needs intervention
- Anterior nares dripping = anterior source (Kiesselbach's)
- Hemodynamically stable, Hgb 13.2 = not exsanguinating
- INR 3.8 = supratherapeutic, contributing to bleeding
- Stepwise: vasoconstrictor → visualize → cautery → packing if needed
- Hold warfarin, consider vitamin K for INR 3.8
- Do NOT tilt head back (causes swallowing blood, aspiration risk)

Evidence: Epistaxis - StatPearls - Tunkel DE, et al. StatPearls. NBK435997. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Immediate posterior packing: Posterior packing is only needed for POSTERIOR bleeds (blood draining down throat, failed anterior measures). This patient has blood dripping from the anterior nares — this is anterior epistaxis. Start with direct pressure, then topical treatment, then anterior cautery/packing.
- Transfuse first: This patient is hemodynamically stable with Hgb 13.2 — no transfusion needed. The INR is supratherapeutic and should be corrected (hold warfarin, vitamin K), but blood products are not indicated for this degree of bleeding.
- Tilt head backward: NEVER tilt head backward in epistaxis. This causes blood to drain down the posterior pharynx, leading to swallowing blood (nausea, vomiting) and aspiration risk. Have the patient lean FORWARD and spit blood out.
- Cauterize both sides: NEVER cauterize both sides of the nasal septum at the same location simultaneously. This creates opposing mucosal injuries that can lead to septal PERFORATION. If bilateral cautery is ever needed, stagger the sites.

Reference: Epistaxis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK435997/ [Accessed 2025]

ACUTE EPIGLOTTITIS — inflammation and swelling of epiglottis and
Severe pharyngitis — oral antibiotics and discharge
Retropharyngeal abscess
Ludwig angina (floor of mouth infection)

explanation:

| Feature | PTA | Epiglottitis | Retropharyngeal |

| Drooling | Mild | SEVERE | Yes |
| Position | Normal | TRIPOD | Neck extended |
| Stridor | No | YES | Possible |
| Exam danger | No | YES — laryngospasm | Less so |
| Imaging | CT neck | Lateral neck (thumbprint) | CT/lateral neck |

Epiglottitis is an AIRWAY EMERGENCY:
• Do NOT examine pharynx aggressively (can trigger complete obstruction)
• Secure airway in CONTROLLED setting (OR with surgical backup)
• Adults: more indolent than children, but still dangerous

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Rapidly progressive sore throat with drooling = supraglottic obstruction
- Tripod position = trying to maintain airway (classic)
- Muffled voice WITHOUT trismus or uvular deviation = NOT PTA
- Stridor = airway compromise (epiglottis obstructing)
- Diabetic = immunocompromised, higher risk
- Do NOT examine aggressively — risk of complete obstruction
- Secure airway in OR with fiberoptic intubation
- IV antibiotics + dexamethasone

Evidence: Epiglottitis - StatPearls - Sauget MM, et al. StatPearls. NBK430960. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- PTA — needle aspiration: PTA has TRISMUS (difficulty opening mouth) and UVULAR DEVIATION — this patient has neither. The pharynx shows no peritonsillar bulging. The combination of drooling, tripod position, stridor, and ABSENCE of PTA findings points to epiglottitis, which has a completely different (and more dangerous) management.
- Pharyngitis — discharge: This is DANGEROUS. Drooling, tripod positioning, and stridor indicate significant airway compromise. Simple pharyngitis does NOT cause these findings. Discharging this patient could result in complete airway obstruction and death.
- Retropharyngeal abscess: Retropharyngeal abscess can cause drooling and airway compromise, but typically occurs in children <6, presents with neck stiffness and refusal to extend neck, and shows retropharyngeal widening on lateral X-ray. This adult presentation with tripod positioning and epiglottic findings is more consistent with epiglottitis.
- Ludwig angina: Ludwig angina is a floor-of-mouth infection causing bilateral submandibular swelling, elevation of the tongue, and difficulty managing secretions. The swelling is visible externally under the jaw. This patient has anterior NECK tenderness with stridor and no submandibular swelling — epiglottitis, not Ludwig angina.

Reference: Epiglottitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430960/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought in by his mother 10 days after being diagnosed with acute otitis media and started on amoxicillin. Despite completing the course, he has developed worsening right ear pain, new-onset fever to 39.8°C, and the mother noticed swelling behind his right ear this morning. On exam, the right auricle is displaced anteriorly and inferiorly. Vital Signs BP 95/60 HR 132 RR 28 Temp 39.8°C What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Periauricular cellulitis
Acute mastoiditis with subperiosteal abscess
Cholesteatoma
Parotitis
Temporomandibular joint dysfunction

explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute mastoiditis is the most common intratemporal complication of acute otitis media. Postauricular erythema, swelling, and anterior/inferior displacement of the pinna are hallmark findings. CT temporal bone is the imaging study of choice. IV antibiotics and myringotomy are first-line; subperiosteal abscess requires surgical drainage (cortical mastoidectomy).

Evidence: Mastoiditis - StatPearls - Loh R, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK554585. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Periauricular cellulitis: Cellulitis would cause erythema without the characteristic pinna displacement and would respond to oral antibiotics.
- Cholesteatoma: Chronic process with keratinized squamous epithelium. Presents with painless otorrhea and hearing loss, not acute fever.
- Parotitis: Swelling anterior to ear at angle of mandible, not posterior. Parotid duct erythema on exam.
- TMJ dysfunction: Pain with jaw movement, clicking, no fever or post-auricular swelling.

Reference: Mastoiditis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554585/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of progressive hoarseness following a total thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid cancer. She also reports occasional aspiration with thin liquids and a breathy voice quality. She denies dyspnea or stridor. Vital Signs BP 128/76 HR 74 RR 16 SpO₂ 98% What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

choices.text: Laryngopharyngeal reflux
Vocal cord nodules from intubation
Unilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury
Subglottic stenosis
Hypothyroidism-related myxedema of vocal cords

explanation:
Correct Answer: Unilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) injury is the most common cause of vocal cord paralysis after thyroidectomy (incidence 1-2% permanent, up to 5% transient). Presents with breathy hoarseness and aspiration of thin liquids. Flexible laryngoscopy is diagnostic. Speech therapy first; medialization thyroplasty if no recovery by 6-12 months.

Evidence: Vocal Cord Paralysis - StatPearls - Mattsson P, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK538297. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Laryngopharyngeal reflux: Chronic throat clearing, globus sensation. Would not cause aspiration of thin liquids or be temporally related to surgery.
- Vocal cord nodules: Develop from chronic voice misuse, not post-surgical. Bilateral, at junction of anterior 1/3 and posterior 2/3.
- Subglottic stenosis: Post-intubation complication but presents with stridor and dyspnea, not hoarseness. Would worsen with exertion.
- Hypothyroidism-related myxedema: Post-thyroidectomy hypothyroidism is possible but vocal cord myxedema causes deeper voice, not breathy quality or aspiration.

Reference: Vocal Cord Paralysis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538297/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 22-year-old man presents with 4 days of worsening sore throat, now with severe left-sided throat pain, difficulty swallowing, and a "hot potato" voice. He has been unable to eat for 24 hours. On exam, the left soft palate is bulging with uvular deviation to the right. He can only open his mouth approximately 2 cm. Vital Signs BP 132/82 HR 108 Temp 39.2°C What finding most reliably distinguishes peritonsillar abscess from peritonsillar cellulitis? Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Degree of fever
Presence of trismus
Aspiration of purulent material from the peritonsillar space
White blood cell count
Response to oral antibiotics within 24 hours

explanation:
Correct Answer: Needle aspiration of purulent material is both diagnostic and therapeutic for peritonsillar abscess (PTA). Clinical features (trismus, uvular deviation, muffled voice) overlap between abscess and cellulitis. CT with contrast can help but aspiration is definitive. PTA requires drainage; cellulitis may respond to IV antibiotics alone.

Evidence: Peritonsillar Abscess Vs Cellulitis - StatPearls - Galioto NJ. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK519503. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Peritonsillar cellulitis: Cellulitis precedes abscess and has similar symptoms but WITHOUT uvular deviation, fluctuance, or trismus. No drainable collection.
- Infectious mononucleosis: Bilateral tonsillar enlargement with exudates, posterior cervical LAD, hepatosplenomegaly. No unilateral bulging.
- Epiglottitis: Sore throat with stridor, dysphagia, drooling but no trismus or unilateral palatal bulging. Thumbprint sign on lateral neck X-ray.
- Ludwig angina: Submandibular/sublingual space infection. Bilateral floor of mouth swelling, tongue elevation. No unilateral palatal findings.

Reference: Peritonsillar Abscess Vs Cellulitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519503/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 62-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history and heavy alcohol use presents with 3 months of progressive hoarseness and a 10-pound unintentional weight loss. He also reports a persistent sensation of "something in his throat" and occasional hemoptysis. Flexible laryngoscopy reveals an exophytic mass on the right true vocal cord with impaired cord mobility. Vital Signs BP 140/88 HR 82 RR 18 What is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.text: Empiric course of proton pump inhibitors for 8 weeks
Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy under general anesthesia
CT neck with contrast and no further evaluation
Voice therapy referral
Observation with repeat laryngoscopy in 3 months

explanation:
Correct Answer: Hoarseness lasting >3 weeks in a patient with tobacco and alcohol exposure requires laryngoscopy. A visible laryngeal mass mandates tissue biopsy via direct laryngoscopy under anesthesia. Squamous cell carcinoma accounts for >90% of laryngeal malignancies. Tobacco and alcohol have synergistic carcinogenic effects.

Evidence: Laryngeal Cancer - StatPearls - Koroulakis A, Agarwal M. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK526076. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Vocal cord polyp: Benign lesion from voice abuse. Smooth, unilateral. Would not cause weight loss or cervical LAD.
- Chronic laryngitis: Diffuse laryngeal inflammation, not a focal lesion. Related to reflux, smoking, voice abuse. No mass effect.
- Reinke edema: Bilateral diffuse polypoid degeneration of vocal cords from smoking. Lower-pitched voice but no focal mass or LAD.
- Laryngeal papillomatosis: HPV-related (types 6, 11). Multiple papillomas, more common in children. Recurrent but rarely malignant.

Reference: Laryngeal Cancer - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526076/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 17-year-old male is brought to the ED after being hit in the face during a basketball game. He has epistaxis controlled with pressure and obvious nasal swelling. On anterior rhinoscopy, you see a smooth, boggy, blue-red swelling obstructing both nasal passages arising from the nasal septum bilaterally. Vital Signs BP 118/72 HR 88 Temp 36.8°C What is the most important immediate intervention?

choices.text: Ice and follow-up in 5-7 days for fracture assessment
CT facial bones before any intervention
Incision and drainage of septal hematoma
Nasal packing for hemostasis
Closed reduction of nasal fracture

explanation:
Correct Answer: Septal hematoma is an ENT emergency requiring immediate incision and drainage. Blood collects between perichondrium and septal cartilage, disrupting the cartilage's blood supply. If untreated, cartilage necrosis occurs within 48-72 hours, leading to septal perforation and saddle nose deformity. Abscess formation (septal abscess) can cause intracranial complications.

Evidence: Nasal Fracture Septal Hematoma - StatPearls - Alshaikh N, Lo S. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK551612. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Observation and ice: Appropriate for simple nasal fracture WITHOUT hematoma, but septal hematoma is a surgical emergency requiring immediate drainage.
- Closed reduction of nasal fracture: Nasal fracture reduction can wait 5-7 days for swelling to resolve. The hematoma requires immediate attention.
- CT facial bones first: Imaging is secondary to drainage. Septal hematoma is a clinical diagnosis that requires immediate intervention.
- Oral antibiotics only: Antibiotics alone will not resolve the hematoma. Undrained hematoma → abscess → cartilage necrosis regardless of antibiotics.

Reference: Nasal Fracture Septal Hematoma - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK551612/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 48-year-old man with BMI 38, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes is referred by his wife for evaluation of loud snoring and witnessed apneic episodes. He reports excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale score 16/24) and morning headaches. He fell asleep while driving last month. Vital Signs BP 152/94 HR 78 BMI 38 Neck 18 inches What is the gold standard diagnostic test, and what metric defines disease severity?

choices.text: Overnight pulse oximetry; oxygen desaturation index
In-laboratory polysomnography; apnea-hypopnea index (AHI)
CT of the upper airway; degree of airway narrowing
Home sleep test; total sleep time
Lateral cephalometric radiograph; posterior airway space

explanation:
Correct Answer: In-laboratory polysomnography (PSG) is the gold standard for OSA diagnosis. AHI defines severity: 5-14 mild, 15-29 moderate, ≥30 severe. Home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) is acceptable for high pretest probability without significant comorbidities. CPAP is first-line therapy for moderate-severe OSA.

Evidence: Osa Screening - StatPearls - Slowik JM, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK459252. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Overnight pulse oximetry alone: Low sensitivity for OSA. Can miss events without desaturation. Not sufficient for diagnosis.
- Lateral cephalometric X-ray: May show anatomic risk factors but does not diagnose OSA. Not a screening or diagnostic test.
- CT of the neck: Not indicated for OSA evaluation. Does not assess dynamic airway collapse during sleep.
- Thyroid function tests: Hypothyroidism can contribute to OSA but TSH alone does not screen for or diagnose OSA.

Reference: Osa Screening - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459252/ [Accessed 2025]
